serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Caprese salad, often served as an appetizer or light main, is approximately 250 grams.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central (FDC IDs: 170889, 170457, 170404, 173571, 170487)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are estimated by combining data for individual ingredients (buffalo mozzarella, tomatoes, basil, olive oil, mesclun) from USDA FoodData Central, scaled to 100g based on an estimated recipe.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database (for individual components)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is estimated based on the low GI values of its primary components: tomatoes (GI ~30), mozzarella (very low GI), and olive oil (GI 0).

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the sodium content in mozzarella and opt for lower-sodium versions or smaller portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Caprese salad is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content, but portion control is still advised.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While olive oil is heart-healthy, the saturated fat content from buffalo mozzarella should be considered for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, suggesting moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caprese Salad is a simple Italian salad, typically prepared with sliced fresh buffalo mozzarella, tomatoes, and sweet basil, seasoned with salt and olive oil. It is usually arranged on a plate in an alternating pattern of mozzarella, tomato, and basil leaves. The dish is served chilled and offers a refreshing taste with creamy, tangy, and herbaceous notes. It is considered a minimally processed food, rich in protein and healthy fats from mozzarella and olive oil, with a low glycemic index. It is naturally vegetarian and gluten-free. Clinically, its low GI makes it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management, but its sodium and saturated fat content warrant moderation for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated by combining data for individual ingredients from USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate based on low GI components. This is not an exact match for a pre-prepared dish.
